Case Study 01 · Healthcare Supply Chain

From product acquisition sprawl
to a cohesive platform

A leading healthcare technology company was losing market share to modern ERP competitors. Leadership believed the platform had the features customers wanted, yet adoption continued to decline. I led a discovery initiative to uncover the root causes, identify unmet user needs, and define a strategy to restore competitive advantage.

Role
UX Design Principal
Domain
Healthcare Supply Chain
Scope
End-to-end · Multi-product
Methods
Systems Thinking · Design System · Interaction Design
40%
Reduction in primary task completion steps across the procurement workflow
6→1
Fragmented product experiences unified into a single cohesive platform
↑ ACV
Positive sales and demo reception, directly supporting annual contract value growth targets
1Context & Problem Space

A suite of products growing in silos, losing ground to modern competitors

My Contributions

  • Led a small team of designers; oversaw end-to-end product design, personally delivered the solution for the Buyer persona — while directing teammates on the Requisitioner, Contract Manager, Vendor, Receiving, and Accounts Payable personas
  • Facilitated and participated in stakeholder and user interviews across requisitioner, buyer, and approver roles
  • Synthesized qualitative research into personas and journey maps
  • Led creation of user flows, storyboards, wireframes, and high-fidelity mockups
  • Partnered with the Fireball Design System team to drive unified visual and interaction standards
  • Influenced product roadmap priorities through design strategy and executive communication

Individual vs. Integrated

Products were acquired and developed by separate teams, each with distinct workflows and visual languages. Users switching between tools experienced constant context disruption.

Confusing Workflows

Features were built without understanding how users actually work. Repetitive, deep-click task flows generated substantial negative feedback from clients and internal product teams.

Inconsistent Aesthetics

Healthcare organizations didn't perceive the suite as a unified platform. Competing visual styles undermined trust and made demos feel disjointed to prospective buyers.

The Fragmented Toolset

Six products a single procurement user might touch in one week — each built by a different team, on a different stack, with no shared design language. This is what "logging in" actually looked like.

"Whenever anyone demos our products it takes…like…four clicks to get somewhere to actually do something… It is embarrassing… nothing looks the same… we are getting our lunch handed to us by our competitors."
VP of Supply Chain Management
2Research & Discovery

Listening to the full procurement ecosystem—from solo practitioners to enterprise teams

We conducted contextual interviews and workflow observations across healthcare organizations of varying sizes. A key insight emerged early: org size fundamentally shapes how users navigate the procure-to-pay journey—a finding that directly informed our persona and workflow architecture.

🏥

Small Orgs: Generalist Buyers

One person handles every phase of procurement—requisition through payment—requiring a streamlined, all-in-one view.

🏢

Large Orgs: Specialized Roles

Higher transaction volume drives specialization. Each role needs a focused, task-specific interface that reduces noise.

🔄

Repetitive Task Overload

Most buyers felt overwhelmed by daily volume. They wanted the system to surface what needs action—not hunt for it.

🎯

Proactive Over Reactive

Users consistently asked for the system to tell them what to do next, rather than requiring manual status checks.

Personas

Six personas emerged from research. As lead, I directed the team across all six — and personally drove end-to-end design for the Buyer, the persona this case study centers on.

Linda Chen
Requisitioner · Regional Clinic Network
Frequent requesterNon-specialistLow system visibility
In her own words
"I know what I need — I just don't always know where to find it fast. Once I submit, it disappears into a black box."
Key pain points
No status update after submitting a request
Generic catalog search not built for her role
Has to ask around just to check on a request
Task frequency
New requests
Catalog search
Status check-ins
Maria Delgado
Buyer · Mid-size Regional HospitalMy Focus
High-volume tasksDaily userNon-specialist
In her own words
"I just want the system to tell me what I need to do. I shouldn't have to hunt around to find my tasks every morning."
Key pain points
Too many clicks to reach everyday tasks
No visibility into where her requests stand
Products look and behave inconsistently
Task frequency
Requisitions
PO tracking
Invoicing
Taylor Brooks
Contract Manager · Large Health System
Intermittent userBudget ownerMobile-first
In his own words
"I approve maybe 15 things a week. Don't make me hunt for the queue — and show me the budget impact before I have to ask someone else."
Key pain points
Approval workflows buried deep in navigation
No context on requisitions — has to chase the buyer
No budget visibility at the moment of approval
Task frequency
Approvals
Spend reports
Requisitions
Pat Alvarez
Vendor · Supply Partner
External stakeholderFulfillment-focusedOff-system visibility
In their own words
"Once the PO comes through, everything happens off to the side. Nobody on their end can see status without calling us directly."
Key pain points
Fulfillment status invisible to the hospital's system
Manual status updates over email and phone
No shared source of truth for order tracking
Task frequency
PO fulfillment
Status updates
Exception handling
Shaun Whitfield
Receiving Coordinator · Community Hospital
Loading dockException-proneManual reconciliation
In his own words
"When something doesn't match, I'm bouncing between three screens just to figure out what actually showed up."
Key pain points
Confirmation loops back manually instead of flowing forward
Exceptions force switching across disconnected screens
No unified trail from PO to confirmed receipt
Task frequency
Delivery confirmation
3-way match
Exception flagging
PN
Priya Nair
AP Team · Community Hospital · Payables
Power userHandles all phasesException-heavy
In her own words
"I touch every part of this process. When products don't connect to each other, I end up doing everything manually to bridge the gaps."
Key pain points
Manual reconciliation due to disconnected product data
Invoice exceptions require switching between 3 screens
No unified audit trail across the full lifecycle
Task frequency
Invoicing
PO matching
Requisitions

Customer Journey Map

Mapping activities, feelings, and expectations across the journey's five personas surfaced where satisfaction actually broke down — most sharply when a requisition escalates for approval, and again at receiving, where confirmation has to loop back manually instead of flowing forward.

Stages of
Journey		 Requisition Escalation & Approval Purchasing & Fulfillment Receiving Payment

Satisfaction
Experiences Simple request, but no visibility once it's submitted Dashboard buried with items that aren't hers to review Escalating means leaving her flow to loop in someone else Has to piece together impact before she can decide Fulfillment happens off-system, completely out of view Confirmation loops back manually instead of flowing forward Reconciles invoices by hand against a process it can't see
Customer
Expectations		 A status update after submitting a request A view scoped to only what she owns Escalation that keeps context and momentum intact Impact analysis surfaced automatically, not assembled by hand Fulfillment status visible without leaving the system One connected trail from PO to confirmed receipt Invoice, PO, and receipt matched automatically

End-to-End Journey: Procure to Pay

Requisition
  • Identify need
  • Search catalog
  • Create req
  • Submit for approval
Approval
  • Review queue
  • Validate budget
  • Approve or reject
  • Route to PO
Purchasing
  • Generate PO
  • Send to vendor
  • Track order status
  • Manage changes
Receiving
  • Confirm delivery
  • 3-way match
  • Flag discrepancies
  • Update inventory
Payment
  • Invoice matching
  • AP review
  • Schedule payment
  • Reconcile GL

Workflow Map

A swimlane view of the same journey, tracing exactly which screen each persona touches and where the process hands off — including the off-system fulfillment gap and the escalation branch that pulls Taylor in mid-flow.

3Design Strategy & Execution

Three strategic shifts to unify the experience at a systems level

01

Persona-Driven Workflows

Tailored available features and navigation to each role's actual responsibilities. Reduced cognitive load by surfacing only what each user needs to accomplish their job—aligned to how they actually work, not how the system was architected.

Systems Thinking
02

Unified Aesthetic via Design System

Partnered with the Fireball Design System team to drive consistent visual vocabulary across all procurement modules. Collaborated with engineering to ensure implementation fidelity—not just design specs on a shelf.

Design System
03

Surface Actions Proactively

Redesigned the dashboard and task flow to prioritize work-to-be-done. Users see a prioritized action queue on arrival—eliminating the "go find it" burden that drove the most negative feedback in research.

Interaction Design

Application Screens

The dashboard, requisitions, analytics, and approval flows shown throughout this case study — the Buyer experience, my primary design focus on this project.

Buyer Dashboard
Requisition Details
Analytics
Contract Spend
Approvals
4Outcomes & Impact

Measurable improvements in usability, consistency, and market confidence

⚡

40% Reduction in Task Steps

Primary procurement workflows were streamlined from deep, multi-click flows to focused, role-appropriate task paths—directly addressing the #1 user pain point.

🎨

Unified Visual Language via Fireball Design System

Consistent interaction patterns and visual components were implemented across all applications, eliminating the fragmented feel that hurt sales demos and user adoption.

📈

Positive Sales & Demo Reception

Sales and demo teams reported strong positive feedback from prospective clients, directly supporting the client's goal of recovering market share from ERP competitors.

🧭

Reduced Navigation Confusion

Role-driven workflows and proactive task surfacing reduced the cognitive burden of navigating complex procurement systems for users across all organization sizes.

What This Project Demonstrates

Systems-level experience design

Unified fragmented multi-product ecosystems into coherent, role-driven customer journeys.

Cross-functional design leadership

Partnered with product, engineering, and design system teams to influence roadmap and implementation quality.

Research-informed strategy

Translated qualitative insights into actionable design principles that shaped both product direction and visual systems.

End-to-end journey ownership

Managed the complete design lifecycle—discovery through high-fidelity delivery—across a complex, multi-touchpoint ecosystem.
